messages.say: Is there a way to use a repeatable deterministic process to treat gate failure as a binary variable and use the same series of all or some of the same self healing files to execute the self heal process. I prefer this to an individual self healing script for each gate

Thought for 29s


According to a document from 2026-02-03, you can make gate failure a binary variable and still run a repeatable, deterministic self-heal loop by separating (1) evaluation from (2) remediation orchestration, and driving remediation from declarative failure modes + reusable healing templates rather than per-gate scripts.


REGISTRY_PLANNING_INTEGRATION_S…


The core pattern


1) Treat every gate outcome as strictly binary


Your plan schema already models gates as discrete objects with commands + pass criteria + evidence, and supports “fail closed” behavior. That naturally makes the gate outcome pass: true|false the primary control signal.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_plan.schema…


Rule: the gate runner emits:


passed = true → continue

passed = false → enter a self-heal loop (no partial credit)


2) Use a single self-heal engine with a mapping, not per-gate logic


Instead of “GATE-001 has its own fixer script,” do:


GateRunner: runs gate → emits evidence + structured failure summary

FailureClassifier: deterministically maps failure → failure_mode_id (based on exit codes, validator codes, evidence signatures)

SelfHealOrchestrator: looks up failure_mode_id → executes a sequence of reusable self-heal steps (templates), then re-runs the same gate(s)


This aligns with your “template-driven execution + self-healing & retry” approach: rerunnable templates, isolated retries, rollback-first discipline, and telemetry.


MULTI_CLI_WORKTREES_EXECUTION_S…


MULTI_CLI_WORKTREES_EXECUTION_S…


Why this stays deterministic (and not “AI guessing”)


A) Failure modes are declarative system artifacts, not code forks


Your governance registry roles explicitly include FAILURE_MODE as a first-class concept (“declarative definition of failure classes and actions”). That’s exactly what you need as the stable “routing table” into reusable heals.


01999000042260124503_REGISTRY_f…


B) Healing steps are reusable templates with strict IO contracts


Model each “heal step” like your other deterministic artifacts: fixed inputs, fixed outputs, and required evidence. This matches your broader insistence on schema-enforced chaining: structured artifact handoff, no free-text drift.


Multiple agents


Recommended structure (minimal but high-signal)


1) GateResult (binary) + Evidence bundle


For each gate run, persist:


gate_id

passed (boolean)

failure_fingerprint (stable hash derived from: exit_code + validator_id + normalized error code set)

evidence_paths[]


(Your plan schema already requires evidence and pass_criteria per gate.)


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_plan.schema…


2) FailureMode registry (declarative)


Define:


failure_mode_id

matchers[] (deterministic predicates over GateResult + evidence metadata)

heal_template_ids[] (ordered list)

max_attempts

cooldown_seconds (optional)

escalation (route to quarantine/human after attempts)


This is the “single source of truth” for what to do when failure looks like X (not tied to one gate).


3) Heal templates (reused across many gates)


Examples of generic heal templates that apply to many gates:


RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE
rollback tag / clean tree / remove failed worktree (if using worktrees)

REGENERATE_DERIVED_ARTIFACTS
re-run deterministic generators

REBUILD_ENV_OR_DEPS
recreate venv / reinstall lockfile deps

REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN
re-materialize plan / patches and re-validate structure

RERUN_GATE
rerun the specific failed gate commands

POST_HEAL_VERIFY
rerun a small “ground truth” set (schema validate, tests, registry validate)


Your worktree spec already frames retries as safe when outputs are isolated and merges are controlled; treat self-heal the same way: fix locally, rerun template, don’t “hand-edit” global state.


MULTI_CLI_WORKTREES_EXECUTION_S…


The deterministic self-heal loop (binary-driven)


For a given gate_id:


Run gate

If passed=true → continue

If passed=false:
classify failure → failure_mode_id

load heal_template_ids[]

execute templates in order (each emits evidence)

rerun the same gate

stop when:
gate passes, or

attempts == max_attempts → escalate (quarantine/human)


This gives you one self-healing system, with a deterministic switchboard (FailureModes) controlling which subset of standard heals run.


What you gain vs per-gate scripts


No script explosion: gates reference shared heals, not bespoke fixers.

Better governance: remediation policy is an auditable artifact (“FAILURE_MODE”), not scattered code paths.
01999000042260124503_REGISTRY_f…

Reusability across projects: gate sets change, but the healing library stays mostly stable.

Fewer “AI decisions”: selection is matcher-driven + schema-validated chaining.
Multiple agents


One non-negotiable constraint


To keep this deterministic, your FailureClassifier must be evidence-first:


It should not “interpret” text.

It should match on stable fields (exit codes, validator IDs, normalized error enums, evidence schema validity).


If you want, the next step is to express this as a strict contract block you can insert into your main plan’s self_healing section (the plan schema explicitly has a self_healing top-level section).


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_plan.schema…


MULTI_CLI_WORKTREES_EXECUTION_S…

Yes, this architecture will work if you maintain these critical dependencies:
What makes it deterministic (and therefore viable)


Stable failure fingerprinting
Your failure_fingerprint (hash of exit_code + validator_id + error codes) only works if:
 ∙    Exit codes are consistent across runs for the same failure class
 ∙    Error codes are normalized enums, not free-text messages
 ∙    Evidence schema validation can reject malformed/unparseable outputs
If a gate’s failure signature varies run-to-run for the same underlying problem, classification breaks.

Heal template idempotence
Templates like RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE or REGENERATE_DERIVED_ARTIFACTS must be safe to run multiple times or in different orders. Your worktree isolation helps here, but templates that modify global state (registry files, shared configs) need explicit idempotence guarantees.

Matcher determinism
Your FailureMode.matchers[] must be pure predicates over the evidence bundle. No:
 ∙    Timestamp-based logic (unless you normalize to “failure within last N runs”)
 ∙    Probabilistic matching
 ∙    External API calls during classification
One structural gap in the proposal
Heal template dependencies/prerequisites: Your ordered heal_template_ids[] assumes linear execution, but some heals may need preconditions. For example:
 ∙    REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN requires a clean state first
 ∙    RERUN_GATE assumes prior heals succeeded
You need either:
 ∙    Explicit precondition checks in templates (emit skipped if prereqs not met)
 ∙    Or DAG-based heal orchestration (probably overkill for v1)
The simpler fix: make templates idempotent enough that ordering doesn’t matter, OR enforce strict sequencing where earlier templates always establish prerequisites for later ones.
Alignment with your existing architecture
This maps cleanly to your governance patterns:
 ∙    FAILURE_MODE = registry artifact (already in your roles)
 ∙    Heal templates = deterministic templates (like your plan execution templates)
 ∙    Evidence-first classification = your schema-enforced artifact handoff
Bottom line: The architecture is sound. Success hinges on fingerprint stability and template idempotence, not on the binary gate model itself.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​


——


Recommended: Strict sequencing + precondition assertions (not skips)
Use ordered heal_template_ids[] with this contract:


Each template returns:


{
  "status": "success" | "failed" | "prereq_violation",
  "evidence_paths": [...],
  "prereq_checks": {  # what this template validated before running
    "clean_worktree": true,
    "schema_valid": true
  }
}


Orchestrator behavior:
	∙	Execute templates in strict order
	∙	If any template returns prereq_violation → fail the entire heal sequence (don’t skip/continue)
	∙	If failed → stop and escalate
	∙	If success → continue to next template
Why this works for your architecture:
	1.	Aligns with your fail-closed discipline: You already model gates as binary pass/fail with “fail closed” behavior. Apply the same to heal sequences—don’t try to recover from prerequisite violations.
	2.	Templates remain simple: Just assert prerequisites at the start:


REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN template


if not is_clean_state(worktree):
    return {"status": "prereq_violation", "message": "requires clean state"}


3.	Explicit sequencing in FailureMode artifact:


{
  "failure_mode_id": "PATCH_CHAIN_CORRUPT",
  "heal_template_ids": [
    "RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE",  // establishes prerequisite
    "REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN",   // asserts clean state
    "RERUN_GATE"
  ]
}


4.	No DAG complexity: You get dependency safety through ordered execution + assertions, not graph resolution.

Adopt your recommendation: strict sequencing + prereq assertions (fail-closed)


That is the right v1. It preserves binary determinism and avoids DAG complexity.


Below is the contract you should standardize across all heal templates and the orchestrator, plus the hard requirements that make the whole thing deterministic.



Determinism invariants (must be enforced)



INV-HEAL-001 Stable failure fingerprint


GateRunner MUST emit:


exit_code (integer)

validator_id (stable ID)

error_codes[] (normalized enums only)

evidence_bundle that is itself schema-valid


Failure fingerprint MUST be computed from normalized fields only:


no free-text messages

no raw stack traces

no timestamps

no absolute paths (unless normalized)


If any of those are violated, classification is not allowed to proceed; it must raise a deterministic failure mode like UNCLASSIFIABLE_FAILURE_SIGNATURE.


INV-HEAL-002 Template idempotence (or explicit state guarantees)


Every template MUST either:


be idempotent, or

declare and prove a state transition that is stable (pre/post conditions + evidence)


If a template mutates shared/global state, it must also:


declare its write-set

emit a post-run signature proving it reached the intended state


INV-HEAL-003 Matcher determinism


Matchers MUST be pure predicates over:


GateResult

failure_fingerprint

schema-validated evidence metadata


No:


external calls

probabilistic matching

clock-time logic (unless reduced to normalized run counters)



Standard heal template result contract (the thing everything shares)



Use exactly this (your format, tightened):


{
  "heal_template_id": "REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN",
  "status": "success|failed|prereq_violation",
  "attempt": 1,
  "prereq_checks": {
    "clean_state": true,
    "schema_valid": true,
    "inputs_present": true
  },
  "evidence_paths": [
    "evidence/heal/REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN/run_0001/result.json"
  ],
  "outputs": {
    "touched_files": [],
    "produced_artifacts": []
  },
  "failure": {
    "error_codes": [],
    "exit_code": 0
  }
}


Rules:


prereq_checks keys are template-specific, but values are always boolean.

failure.error_codes[] must be normalized enums.

failed and prereq_violation must be distinguishable (you already called this out correctly).



Orchestrator runtime rules (strict order, fail-closed)



Orchestrator algorithm


Given a failed gate:


Classify → failure_mode_id

Load ordered heal_template_ids[]

For each template in order:
run prereq checks (inside template)

if prereq_violation → stop; mark heal sequence failed; escalate

if failed → stop; escalate

if success → continue

After all templates succeed → rerun the gate(s)

Gate passes → resume pipeline

Gate fails again → repeat until max_attempts then escalate


Orchestrator must also enforce:


attempt ceilings at both levels:
per failure_mode

per template (optional but useful)

no partial continuation after prereq violations

evidence required for every template run, even failures



FailureMode artifact shape (ordered sequencing + attempts)



Use this structure:


{
  "failure_mode_id": "PATCH_CHAIN_CORRUPT",
  "matchers": [
    { "validator_id": "PATCH_VALIDATOR", "error_codes_any": ["PATCH_CHAIN_INVALID"] }
  ],
  "heal_sequence": {
    "max_attempts": 2,
    "heal_template_ids": [
      "RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE",
      "REAPPLY_PATCH_CHAIN",
      "RERUN_GATE"
    ]
  },
  "escalation": {
    "action": "quarantine",
    "required_evidence": ["heal_sequence_report.json", "gate_result.json"]
  }
}


Key point: keep heal_sequence explicitly scoped as an ordered sequence so nothing “helpfully reorders” it.



Prereq assertions (the simple dependency fix you want)



Each template must start with prereq validation and return prereq_violation if not met.


Example prereqs you should standardize across templates (high-signal set):


clean_state (no uncommitted changes / worktree matches baseline)

schema_valid (inputs and registries validate)

inputs_present (required files exist)

write_set_lock_acquired (if you enforce write locks)

prior_template_success (optional, usually redundant if ordered)


This gives you dependency safety without a DAG.



The one extra thing you should add: “unclassifiable” is a first-class failure mode



You already understand why: if fingerprint stability fails, the whole system stops being deterministic.


So define a reserved failure mode:


UNCLASSIFIABLE_FAILURE_SIGNATURE

heal sequence:
CAPTURE_DIAGNOSTIC_EVIDENCE (deterministic packaging)

RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE

RERUN_GATE

if still unclassifiable → escalate immediately


This prevents silent drift into “AI interpretation.”



If you want this hardened further, the next deterministic step is to require a schema for FailureMode, a schema for HealTemplateResult, and a HealSequenceReport schema that aggregates the ordered results and produces a single healed: true|false output for downstream gates.

Develop the next deterministic step a require a schema for FailureMode, a schema for HealTemplateResult, and a HealSequenceReport schema that aggregates the ordered results and produces a single healed: true|false output for downstream gates.

To at your response, Is there a pattern that can be reused ?


for every gate with only small  gate specific changes to the pattern input received and output delivered

Yes. Use a single reusable pattern: Gate Spec → Gate Run → Gate Result → (optional) Heal Sequence → Final Gate Verdict. Only the gate adapter changes per gate (inputs, command, evidence extractors). Everything else is identical.


Reusable pattern (the invariant pipeline)


A) Inputs (per-gate config only)


Each gate supplies a GateSpec (per gate instance):


gate_id

validator_id

inputs (paths, ids, parameters)

commands[] (how to run)

evidence_contract (what evidence must be produced + schema ids)

pass_criteria (binary)

failure_fingerprint_contract (which normalized fields contribute)


Everything above is “data”, not code.


B) Execution (shared runner)


A shared GateRunner does the same steps for every gate:


Acquire locks (if required)

Run commands[]

Collect raw outputs

Normalize → error_codes[], exit_code, validator_id

Validate evidence schemas

Emit GateResult (binary passed: true|false) + failure_fingerprint


C) Remediation (shared self-heal)


If passed=false:


Classify → failure_mode_id via deterministic matchers

Execute ordered heal_template_ids[]

Produce HealSequenceReport with healed: true|false

If healed=true → rerun gate → emit final verdict

If healed=false → escalate


D) Outputs (stable across all gates)


Every gate produces the same output set:


GateResult.json

(optional) HealSequenceReport.json

evidence bundle(s) referenced by paths in the above


Only the content of inputs and evidence changes per gate.



What varies per gate (small, bounded differences)


1) Gate adapter (the only gate-specific “module”)


For each gate you define:


Input shape: which files/ids/registries it consumes

Command recipe: command list + environment assumptions

Evidence extractor: how raw tool output maps to normalized error_codes[]

Evidence schemas used: which schema IDs validate the evidence artifacts

Pass criteria: still binary, but specific to the gate’s validator outputs


Everything else is shared.


2) Failure modes mapping (data-only changes)


You may add:


new FailureMode objects

new matcher conditions

new heal_sequence order


No per-gate healing scripts.



Concrete reusable “interface” to standardize


GateSpec (pattern)


{
  "gate_id": "GATE_SCHEMA_VALIDATE_PLAN",
  "validator_id": "JSONSCHEMA_VALIDATOR",
  "inputs": {
    "plan_path": "plans/main.plan.json",
    "schema_id": "schemas/NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_plan.schema.v3.0.0.json"
  },
  "commands": [
    {
      "tool": "jsonschema",
      "args": ["-i", "{{inputs.plan_path}}", "{{inputs.schema_id}}"]
    }
  ],
  "evidence_contract": {
    "required_artifacts": [
      {
        "kind": "validator_report",
        "path": "evidence/gates/{{gate_id}}/validator_report.json",
        "schema_id": "schemas/validator_report.schema.json"
      }
    ]
  },
  "fingerprint_contract": {
    "fields": ["exit_code", "validator_id", "error_codes"]
  }
}


GateResult (pattern output)


{
  "gate_id": "GATE_SCHEMA_VALIDATE_PLAN",
  "validator_id": "JSONSCHEMA_VALIDATOR",
  "passed": false,
  "exit_code": 2,
  "error_codes": ["SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED"],
  "failure_fingerprint": "sha256:...",
  "evidence_paths": [
    "evidence/gates/GATE_SCHEMA_VALIDATE_PLAN/validator_report.json"
  ]
}


Optional HealSequenceReport (pattern output)


Always identical schema; only values differ.



The reusable pattern name (so you can codify it)


Use a single contract called:


“Binary Gate + Deterministic Remediation Loop (BG-DRL)”


Define it once in your technical spec and require every gate to implement it via:


GateSpec (gate-specific)

shared GateRunner + SelfHealOrchestrator

shared schemas (GateResult, HealTemplateResult, HealSequenceReport, FailureMode)



Practical rule to keep “small gate-specific changes” true


A gate is allowed to customize only these five fields:


inputs

commands

evidence_contract

error_code_normalization mapping (tool output → enums)

pass_criteria (still binary)


If a gate needs more customization than that, it’s a sign you’re missing a reusable template or a normalized error taxonomy.


If you want the next deterministic step: define a single gate_spec.schema.json and a gate_result.schema.json so GateRunner consumes/produces identical shapes for every gate, and gate variability remains confined to inputs/commands/evidence.
Can the same concept be applied to the automation that moves the plan data from one gate/phase to the next

Yes. Treat phase-to-phase handoff exactly like gates: a single reusable deterministic transport pattern where only the phase adapter changes (inputs accepted, outputs produced).


Reusable pattern


“Artifact Envelope + Handoff Contract + State Transition”.


1) Every phase consumes the same wrapper, not raw files


Define one canonical wrapper object (call it ArtifactEnvelope) that carries:


identity (run_id, plan_id, phase_id, attempt)

normalized inputs (artifact references, not inline blobs)

evidence references

snapshot signature(s)

provenance (what produced what)


A phase is only allowed to read from the envelope + referenced artifacts.


2) Every phase emits the same wrapper


Each phase produces:


PhaseResult (binary: advanced=true|false)

updated ArtifactEnvelope (new artifacts appended, old retained)

HandoffReport (schema-valid) that becomes the next phase’s input


3) The orchestrator is a deterministic state machine


The orchestrator does the same thing for every phase:


Validate envelope schema

Validate required artifacts and evidence (per phase contract)

Run the phase’s commands/templates

Validate produced artifacts against declared schemas

Compute snapshot signature(s)

Emit PhaseResult + HandoffReport

Route to next phase strictly by next_phase_id (declared in the plan)


No “special case” phase logic.


What varies per phase (small bounded changes)


Each phase only customizes:


Required inputs: artifact kinds + schema_ids it needs

Commands/templates: what runs

Produced outputs: artifact kinds + schema_ids it must emit

Advance criteria: still binary (advance or stop)

Write-set/locks: what it is allowed to touch


Everything else is shared.



The minimal contracts you need (data-only)


A) phase_contract (per phase)


Defines what the phase requires and what it must produce.


{
  "phase_id": "PHASE_VALIDATE",
  "requires": [
    { "kind": "plan", "schema_id": "schemas/plan.schema.json" },
    { "kind": "gate_catalog", "schema_id": "schemas/gate_catalog.schema.json" }
  ],
  "produces": [
    { "kind": "gate_results_bundle", "schema_id": "schemas/gate_results_bundle.schema.json" },
    { "kind": "handoff_report", "schema_id": "schemas/handoff_report.schema.json" }
  ],
  "advance_rule": { "type": "binary", "field": "phase_passed", "must_equal": true },
  "write_policy": { "write_set_id": "WS_VALIDATE" }
}


B) ArtifactEnvelope (always the same)


References artifacts by path + hash + schema_id so downstream consumption is deterministic.


{
  "run_id": "RUN_20260208_001",
  "plan_id": "PLAN_MAIN",
  "phase_id": "PHASE_VALIDATE",
  "attempt": 1,
  "artifacts": [
    {
      "kind": "plan",
      "path": "plans/main.plan.json",
      "sha256": "…",
      "schema_id": "schemas/plan.schema.json"
    }
  ],
  "evidence_paths": [
    "evidence/phase/PHASE_VALIDATE/preflight.json"
  ],
  "snapshot_signature": {
    "inputs_sig": "sha256:…",
    "artifacts_sig": "sha256:…"
  }
}


C) HandoffReport (always the same shape)


This is the deterministic “move data to next phase” object.


{
  "from_phase_id": "PHASE_VALIDATE",
  "to_phase_id": "PHASE_EXECUTE",
  "phase_passed": true,
  "advanced": true,
  "envelope_path": "state/envelopes/RUN_20260208_001/PHASE_VALIDATE.out.json",
  "required_artifacts_present": true,
  "produced_artifacts_valid": true,
  "termination_reason": "phase_success"
}


Downstream logic uses only:


advanced (boolean)

to_phase_id

envelope_path


Deterministic routing rule


Phase progression is a pure function of:


current phase_id

advanced boolean

the plan’s declared phase_graph (or ordered list)


No “if this phase, do this special thing”.


Add one guardrail to avoid drift


Require that every phase declares:


requires[] and produces[] with schema_id

and the orchestrator enforces:
missing required inputs → fail closed

any produced artifact fails schema validation → fail closed

envelope signature mismatch → fail closed



Bottom line


Yes: the same concept applies cleanly. Gates are “micro-transitions.” Phases are “macro-transitions.” Both become instances of the same reusable pattern:


Deterministic Contracted Handoff (DCH):


envelope in

validate

run

validate outputs

envelope out

single boolean controls routing


If you want the next step, it’s to define three schemas mirroring what you did for self-heal:


artifact_envelope.schema.json

phase_contract.schema.json

handoff_report.schema.json


That locks the phase-to-phase automation into the same deterministic, reusable shape as your gates.

This defines two solid reusable patterns that align well with your scan-based SSOT architecture. Here’s targeted feedback:
Strengths:
	1.	Clean separation of concerns: Gate/phase adapters (data) vs. shared runners (code) mirrors your overlay-file approach where automation handles structure and humans add semantics
	2.	Fingerprinting for determinism: The failure_fingerprint concept directly supports your goal of preventing documentation drift - same input state = same fingerprint = idempotent remediation
	3.	Schema-driven validation: Requiring schema_id for all artifacts creates enforcement points that prevent the “stale docs” problem you identified
Gaps to address:
	1.	Missing provenance chain: Your ArtifactEnvelope has provenance but doesn’t specify how it links artifacts across phases. Consider adding:
	∙	produced_by_gate_id or produced_by_phase_id
	∙	derived_from_artifacts[] with references to parent artifacts
	∙	This creates audit trails for “why did this file change?”
	2.	Snapshot signature collision: The snapshot_signature uses both inputs_sig and artifacts_sig, but it’s unclear:
	∙	What happens when inputs don’t change but process does?
	∙	How do you distinguish “same inputs, different process version” from “truly identical”?
	∙	Suggest adding process_version or gate_spec_hash to the signature components
	3.	Phase contract validation timing: You specify “validate required artifacts” at orchestrator level, but what happens if:
	∙	A phase declares it produces artifact X but doesn’t?
	∙	A phase produces undeclared artifacts?
	∙	Suggest explicit strict_mode: true flag with fail-closed behavior
	4.	Heal template composability: The heal_template_ids[] assumes ordered execution, but what about:
	∙	Templates that are mutually exclusive?
	∙	Templates with dependencies on each other?
	∙	Consider adding heal_template_dag instead of flat list
Integration with your current work:
Since you’re building scan_repo.py, consider adding a gate early:


GATE_FILESYSTEM_SCAN_COMPLETE


validates scan produced required overlay structure

fingerprint includes filesystem hash

enables self-heal if scan incomplete


This would let you test the BG-DRL pattern on your immediate blocker.
One critical question: How do these patterns handle concurrent execution? Your file mentions “acquire locks” but doesn’t specify the locking protocol. If two gates need to write overlays simultaneously, who wins?​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Close the provenance-chain gap (auditability across phases)



Reusable pattern: Lineage links are mandatory on every artifact reference


Extend ArtifactEnvelope.artifacts[] entries with producer + parents:


{
  "kind": "gate_results_bundle",
  "path": "state/gates/GATE_SCHEMA_VALIDATE_PLAN/result.json",
  "sha256": "...",
  "schema_id": "schemas/gate_results_bundle.schema.json",

  "provenance": {
    "produced_by": {
      "type": "gate|phase|heal_template",
      "id": "GATE_SCHEMA_VALIDATE_PLAN",
      "run_id": "RUN_20260208_001",
      "attempt": 1
    },
    "derived_from_artifacts": [
      { "kind": "plan", "path": "plans/main.plan.json", "sha256": "..." }
    ]
  }
}


Deterministic rule: every produced artifact MUST declare derived_from_artifacts[] as the exact set of input artifact references used (by path+hash). This answers “why did this file change?” without human interpretation.



Fix snapshot signature collisions (same inputs, different process)



Reusable pattern: Signature = Inputs + Artifacts + Process identity


You need to explicitly bind execution to a process identity so “same inputs, different runner/spec” becomes detectable.


Add these fields to the envelope and signature:


process_identity.runner_version (semantic version of orchestrator)

process_identity.spec_hashes.gate_spec_hash and/or phase_contract_hash

process_identity.template_catalog_hash (hash of heal template registry, if relevant)


Example:


{
  "process_identity": {
    "runner_version": "3.1.0",
    "gate_spec_hash": "sha256:...",
    "phase_contract_hash": "sha256:...",
    "failure_mode_catalog_hash": "sha256:...",
    "heal_template_catalog_hash": "sha256:..."
  },
  "snapshot_signature": {
    "inputs_sig": "sha256:...",
    "artifacts_sig": "sha256:...",
    "process_sig": "sha256:...", 
    "snapshot_sig": "sha256:..." 
  }
}


Deterministic rule:
snapshot_sig = hash(inputs_sig + artifacts_sig + process_sig)
So you can distinguish:


identical state + identical process (true replay)

identical state + changed process (expected drift due to version change)



Make phase contract enforcement fail-closed (declared vs actual outputs)



Reusable pattern: Strict declared IO + strict mode


Add strict_mode: true to every PhaseContract (default true).


PhaseContract:


{
  "phase_id": "PHASE_VALIDATE",
  "strict_mode": true,
  "requires": [...],
  "produces": [...],
  "allow_extra_outputs": false
}


Orchestrator enforcement (deterministic):


If any requires[] missing → fail closed

If any declared produces[] missing after phase runs → fail closed

If any produced artifact not declared and allow_extra_outputs=false → fail closed

If any produced artifact fails schema validation → fail closed


Output should record a binary phase_passed and an enum termination reason:


missing_required_inputs

missing_declared_outputs

undeclared_outputs_produced

output_schema_invalid

phase_success



Heal template composability (mutual exclusivity + dependencies)



You have two deterministic options. Pick one.


Option A (recommended v1): keep ordered list, add “exclusive groups”


No DAG. Still handles “mutually exclusive templates” cleanly.


FailureMode:


{
  "heal_sequence": {
    "heal_template_ids": ["RESET_TO_CLEAN_STATE", "RETRY_DEP_INSTALL", "RERUN_GATE"],
    "exclusive_groups": [
      {
        "group_id": "ENV_FIX",
        "members": ["RETRY_DEP_INSTALL", "REBUILD_ENV"],
        "selection_rule": { "mode": "first_success" }
      }
    ]
  }
}


Deterministic execution rule:


execute group members in listed order

stop at first success

if all fail → sequence fails


Option B (v2): DAG


Only do this if you truly need parallel or complex prerequisites. Otherwise it’s governance overhead. If you implement DAG, it must still be deterministic:


topological order must be stable (tie-breaker = template_id sort)

prereq violations fail closed unless explicitly marked “optional”



Concurrency: define the locking protocol (who wins)



Reusable pattern: Write-Set Locks with a deterministic lock manager


Your “who wins?” question is decided by a single invariant: no two concurrent steps may overlap in write-set.


A) Every gate/phase/heal template declares a write-set


Write-set is a list of:


file IDs (preferred)

paths/directories (fallback)

registry keys (semantic keys)


Example (data-only):


{
  "write_set": {
    "file_ids": ["01999000042260124503"],
    "paths": ["registries/UNIFIED_SSOT_REGISTRY.json"],
    "semantic_keys": ["REGISTRY:UNIFIED_SSOT:ROWSET"]
  }
}


B) Lock acquisition is deterministic


Lock manager rules:


Normalize write-set (canonical sort)

Compute lock_id = hash(canonical_write_set)

Acquire locks in canonical order (prevents deadlock)

Use a lease with heartbeat (lease_seconds)

If lock is held, resolve by deterministic policy:
default: FIFO by requested_at (monotonic counter from orchestrator, not wall clock), tie-breaker run_id

or: priority by phase order + gate priority, tie-breaker run_id


C) What happens if two gates want same lock?


One blocks. There is no “winner” that proceeds concurrently. Determinism > throughput.


D) Parallelism is only allowed when write-sets are disjoint


So your scheduler can run gates concurrently iff:
intersection(write_set(A), write_set(B)) == ∅


This integrates cleanly with worktrees:


worktree-local writes are disjoint by construction

shared registries/global overlays are protected by write-set locks



Your suggested early gate: GATE_FILESYSTEM_SCAN_COMPLETE (fits perfectly)



Yes. Make it the first gate in the chain to validate:


scan produced required overlay structure

scan output schema-valid

scan snapshot signature computed

filesystem hash captured (normalized)


Fingerprint inputs:


validator_id = SCAN_VALIDATOR

error_codes = ["SCAN_MISSING_OVERLAYS", "SCAN_SCHEMA_INVALID", ...]

plus a deterministic filesystem_state_sig captured as evidence (not directly in error text)


Self-heal targets:


rerun scan

rebuild overlay skeleton

regenerate missing derived indexes



Direct answer to your critical question


Concurrency is handled deterministically by declared write-sets + lock manager. If two gates need to write overlays simultaneously and the write-sets overlap, they do not run simultaneously. The one that proceeds is chosen by a deterministic queue/priority rule; the other waits.

Excellent - these solutions close the gaps deterministically. A few refinements:
Provenance chain:
The derived_from_artifacts[] binding is correct, but add one field:


"provenance": {
  "transformation_rule": "gate_spec_id|heal_template_id|phase_command_hash"
}


This lets you replay exactly what process transformed inputs → outputs, not just “who ran.”
Process signature:
Good fix. One edge case: what if runner_version changes but logic is identical? Consider:


"process_identity": {
  "semantic_version": "3.1.0",
  "logic_hash": "sha256:..."  // hash of actual execution logic
}


So cosmetic version bumps don’t invalidate fingerprints unnecessarily.
Strict mode:
The fail-closed enforcement is solid. Add one safety:


"validation_evidence_path": "evidence/phase/PHASE_X/io_validation.json"


So you can audit why strict mode failed without re-running.
Heal template exclusivity:
Option A (exclusive groups) is the right v1 choice. The first_success rule is deterministic. Consider adding:


"exclusive_groups": [{
  "fallback_if_all_fail": "ESCALATE|CONTINUE|FAIL_PHASE"
}]


Write-set locks:
The deterministic ordering + FIFO resolution works. Critical addition needed:


"write_set": {
  "lock_timeout_seconds": 300,
  "on_timeout": "FAIL_CLOSED|FORCE_RELEASE"
}


Otherwise a crashed holder blocks forever.
GATE_FILESYSTEM_SCAN_COMPLETE:
Perfect integration point. The fingerprint should include:
	∙	Scan schema version (so schema changes trigger re-validation)
	∙	Expected overlay file count (so missing overlays are detectable)
One remaining question: How do derived_from_artifacts[] hashes get validated? If parent artifact changes after child was produced, do you detect the orphan?
Suggest adding to orchestrator: before accepting an artifact, verify all derived_from_artifacts[] sha256 values still match current filesystem state. Fail closed on mismatch.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Apply refinements (all deterministic, no new degrees of freedom)


1) Provenance: add transformation_rule


Add to every produced artifact’s provenance:


"provenance": {
  "produced_by": { "type": "gate|phase|heal_template", "id": "GATE_X", "run_id": "RUN...", "attempt": 1 },
  "derived_from_artifacts": [ ... ],
  "transformation_rule": "gate_spec_id|heal_template_id|phase_command_hash"
}


Deterministic rule: transformation_rule MUST be one of:


gate_spec_id (stable ID of the gate spec instance)

heal_template_id

phase_command_hash (hash of the exact command list + env contract for the phase)



2) Process identity: split cosmetic version from logic identity


Replace/extend process identity with:


"process_identity": {
  "semantic_version": "3.1.0",
  "logic_hash": "sha256:..."
}


Deterministic rule: logic_hash is computed from the canonicalized runner logic package manifest (or equivalent immutable build fingerprint). Cosmetic bumps change semantic_version only; functional changes change logic_hash.


Signature becomes:


process_sig = hash(logic_hash + gate_spec_hash + phase_contract_hash + catalogs_hashes...)

snapshot_sig = hash(inputs_sig + artifacts_sig + process_sig)



3) Strict mode: require validation evidence


In PhaseContract (or per-phase config), add:


"validation_evidence_path": "evidence/phase/PHASE_X/io_validation.json"


Deterministic enforcement: On any strict-mode failure (missing_required_inputs, missing_declared_outputs, undeclared_outputs_produced, output_schema_invalid), orchestrator MUST emit this evidence artifact and reference it from the PhaseResult/HandoffReport.



4) Heal exclusivity: add fallback policy


Extend exclusive_groups[]:


"exclusive_groups": [{
  "group_id": "ENV_FIX",
  "members": ["RETRY_DEP_INSTALL", "REBUILD_ENV"],
  "selection_rule": { "mode": "first_success" },
  "fallback_if_all_fail": "ESCALATE|CONTINUE|FAIL_PHASE"
}]


Deterministic enforcement: fallback_if_all_fail is executed exactly as declared; default should be ESCALATE (fail-closed posture).



5) Write-set locks: add timeout + timeout policy


Extend every write_set declaration:


"write_set": {
  "file_ids": ["..."],
  "paths": ["..."],
  "semantic_keys": ["..."],
  "lock_timeout_seconds": 300,
  "on_timeout": "FAIL_CLOSED|FORCE_RELEASE"
}


Deterministic rule (recommended):


default on_timeout = FAIL_CLOSED

FORCE_RELEASE allowed only if:
you have a lease/heartbeat model, and

you can prove holder is dead (no heartbeat past lease), and

force release emits a mandatory evidence record


This prevents “stuck forever” without introducing nondeterministic operator judgment.



6) GATE_FILESYSTEM_SCAN_COMPLETE fingerprint additions


Add to the fingerprint input components (normalized evidence fields):


scan_schema_id or scan_schema_version (enum/string)

expected_overlay_file_count (integer)

observed_overlay_file_count (integer)


Keep the fingerprint definition strict: it hashes normalized fields, not raw text.



Orphan detection: validating derived_from_artifacts[] hashes


Yes—make it a required orchestrator pre-acceptance check.


Reusable pattern: Lineage Integrity Verification (LIV)


Before accepting any artifact into the envelope (or before advancing phase), orchestrator must:


For each artifact A being accepted:

For each parent reference P in A.provenance.derived_from_artifacts[]:
verify P.path exists

compute current sha256(P.path)

compare to recorded P.sha256

If any mismatch:
mark artifact as orphaned

fail closed: advanced=false

emit termination_reason="lineage_mismatch"

write validation evidence to the phase’s validation_evidence_path


What this guarantees


If a parent changes after the child was produced, you detect it deterministically.

Prevents silent drift where downstream phases consume an artifact whose lineage no longer matches reality.


Optional enhancement (still deterministic)


Add an artifact-level boolean:


lineage_verified: true|false
and require it be true for any artifact that is used as an input to a later phase/gate.



Minimal schema diffs you should apply next


ArtifactEnvelope / artifact reference schema:
add provenance.transformation_rule

Process identity schema:
add semantic_version, logic_hash

PhaseContract schema:
add validation_evidence_path

FailureMode schema:
extend exclusive_groups with fallback_if_all_fail

Write-set schema:
add lock_timeout_seconds, on_timeout

Orchestrator invariant:
add LIV check as a required pre-advance gate in every phase


These changes preserve your two reusable patterns and harden them into a replayable, drift-detecting SSOT pipeline.
